messages.author: expensive
messages.content:
Gradient-free adaptation of frozen 1-bit language models via discrete search over binary weight groups.
Check-out the Colab notebook for a proof-of-concept of post-deployment adaptation on Bonsai 1.7B. A 13 KB patch (3,019 sign-group flips, 0.023% of weights) corrects two verbatim text extraction failures. You can run it for free on T4 after waiting for Bonsai to download, applying the patch itself happens in almost instantly.
The patch was found by a targeted search that concentrates on probes nearest the decision boundary (focal-loss-style weighting). The search crossed both targeted probe boundaries in around 6,000 iterations.
Patch searches can be performed relatively cheaply or on a home setup in 20 hours or less.
Colab: https://colab.research.google.com/drive/11ozwjKPSCNewuoR9w9ziHs1oJUpy0d8U
Github w/ paper: https://github.com/sbenjam1n/Neagari (edited) Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10:15 AM

messages.author: Dark_Sca
messages.content:
Hey everyone - Trillim now supports Ternary Bonsai in v0.9.0 .
We added support with optimized CPU paths for both x86 AVX2 and Arm NEON. If you want to run Ternary Bonsai locally through the usual Trillim flow, it’s available now.
Install with pip install trillim or uv add trillim
Blog post with benchmarks: https://trillim.com/blog/trillim-supports-ternary-bonsai/
If you run into issues, feel free to DM me

messages.author: twoxfh
messages.content: I am having a hard time finding more information about trillim and if anyone has used it? .. looks like its strength is scaling with multiple threads..

messages.author: Dark_Sca
messages.content:
We're a couple of college kids trying to build the fastest local AI stack that works on consumer hardware
AI inference in general scales with multiple threads coz it is full of matrix multiplications which is an extremely parallel workload - that's why GPUs sell like hot cakes
I can help you get set up if you'd like

messages.author: twoxfh
messages.content: @Dark_Sca trying to figure out what the dependencies are and exactly what is DarkNet?

messages.author: Dark_Sca
messages.content:
DarkNet is our proprietary C++ inference runtime - it is highly optimized C++ code that runs the models
Trillim is the Python package around it - it provides interfaces to use LLMs executed by DarkNet and other components like speech to text with whisper and text to speech with pocket tts

messages.author: Dark_Sca
messages.content: The python package is open source - https://github.com/Trillim/Trillim

messages.author: twoxfh
messages.content: Ahh proprietary.. thats a deal breaker for many..

messages.author: twoxfh
messages.content: Looks really cool though

messages.author: Dark_Sca
messages.content: Hmm - is that all that's stopping you from giving it a try?

messages.author: Dark_Sca
messages.content: Anything else we can improve?

messages.author: twoxfh
messages.content: Thats the major barrier for me. I like to see the whole things soup to nuts with a scanner and infer with confidence

messages.author: Dark_Sca
messages.content: Anyway I can change your mind? We're not looking to open source it any time soon because we're not sure where it could be useful.

messages.author: Dark_Sca
messages.content: If you're really paranoid, you could run it in a sandbox without network permissions - since it is fully local, it will work...

messages.author: twoxfh
messages.content: Sounds like your market is enterprise usecases.. My usecases are home/personal data..

messages.author: Dark_Sca
messages.content:
understandable, but we built Trillim to work offline for exactly that reason
if you ever want to give it a try in the future you can DM me and I'll be happy to help

messages.author: KitaSan
messages.content:
I just released version 0.1.1 of OxiBonsai, a Pure Rust version of the Bonsai,
https://github.com/cool-japan/oxibonsai
(version 0.1.0 only supported 1-bit), so I'd like to show it to you.
With Ternary-Bonsai, using a MacBook Pro M3 (I was working on other development projects simultaneously, so I think it would actually be faster), I think it can achieve around 50 tokens/sec.

messages.author: Chaika
messages.content:
Hello everyone ! Ive recently started building user friendly interfaces for Bonsai models and Prism based LLMs :) i really enjoy this project and i want it to be really accessible for people.
So here are the two projects i've currently released on github, the first is Bonsaidesk, a beginner friendly web ui that lets you chat with Bonsai models very easily, and customise the inferences as well.
https://github.com/Kxrbx/BonsaiDesk
The second one is BonsaiCLI a terminal based chat interface for Bonsai models, which still needs some fixing. https://github.com/Kxrbx/BonsaiCLI
Theyre both made to be ran locally and be easy to use for beginner users :)
Im planning some updates to both of them and i have a bigger project in the work for end users, that also uses bonsai under the hood. It's all open to contribution so yeah dont hesitate ! (edited) Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 7:19 PM

messages.author: KitaSan
messages.content:
@Frai Beria @Evan
Pushed v0.1.3 which fixes the Windows 100% CPU hang during token generation.
Root cause was in scirs2-core: the CPU feature detection was misdetecting SIMD capabilities on Windows AMD CPUs (AVX2/AVX-512 fallback was broken → scalar path). Switched to Rust’s native is_x86_feature_detected! and it’s now correctly picking the right kernel.
Issue #4 closed: https://github.com/cool-japan/oxibonsai/issues/4
Could you try the latest release and let me know if it’s resolved? (master branch)
If it’s still happening, I’d really appreciate:
your CPU model ,
exact command you’re running ,
RUST_LOG=debug output if possible ,
(Unfortunately I don’t have a Windows/AMD test env yet)
Also, I’m working on AMD ROCm support in parallel — if PrismML has a test environment I could borrow, I’d love to collaborate on that. (edited) Monday, May 4, 2026 at 3:10 PM

messages.author: Frai Beria
messages.content: Perfect, do you have any plans for GPU support?

messages.author: Evan
messages.content: awesome!

messages.author: KitaSan
messages.content:
@Frai Beria
Glad to hear the CPU issue is resolved!
Yes — GPU support is already there:
Apple Silicon: Full Metal backend (fused Q1 + TQ2 kernels, single command buffer) ,
NVIDIA: Native CUDA backend (NVRTC) + scirs2-core CUDA path ,
Both are giving solid performance right now.
For AMD (ROCm), I'm actively working on a Pure-Rust PoC in parallel.
If PrismML has any ROCm test environment I could use (even temporarily), I'd love to get access — it would speed things up a lot.
Anything specific on the GPU side you'd like me to prioritize?

messages.author: chaiwithjai
messages.content:
Because Fable 5 got pulled last week, I wanted to map some of my work to the intellectual density framework. I wanted to create a case study for intellectual density for hospitals and major clinics.
The case study is specifically for pulmonary health and reducing patient discharges that lead to readmissions. Case studies help people who still can’t imagine what AI can do and many organizations require 50+ case studies to be generated because of how massive they are.
The goal of this case study was seeing how we could create institutional memory/intelligence using only Bonsai. This demo was nearly autonomously created using a loop engineering system that I demo the document for at the end.
Healthcare demo: https://www.loom.com/share/fee10977c3ce46a39143c5e196710796
Github: https://github.com/ChaiWithJai/pneumonia-discharge-memory

messages.author: Alensoft
messages.content:
Alenface: A Lightweight Approach to Local LLMs on macOS
I’ve been working on a project to bridge the gap between complex machine learning tools and everyday users. Many of my friends and family are curious about trying out Bonsai , but the technical hurdles—like managing Docker containers or navigating the complexities of Llama models—often stand in their way.
To make matters more challenging, many of them are still using older Mac M1 hardware. I wanted to create a solution that allows anyone, regardless of their technical background or hardware limitations, to experience these models without the setup overhead.
I developed Alenface to simplify this process. It functions similarly to LM Studio but is significantly more lightweight, relying on optimized Llama implementations and a native interface to ensure it remains responsive even on older M1 machines.
Check it out here: https://alensoft.com/
I’m curious to hear your thoughts on this approach:
How effective do you think this is for lowering the barrier to entry for non-technical users? ,
Are there specific areas where this implementation could be refined for better performance on Apple Silicon? ,
